narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which is above the typical student's 3.21 average. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated your probability of failing at 47.9%—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application. The two biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance wasn't as competitive as most admitted students'. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 32.5, slightly under the average of 36.7, actually worked in your favor more strongly than that, nudging the prediction toward success more than any other single factor. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the typical 5.7, added some additional concern for the model, as did your middle family income bracket compared to higher averages in the dataset. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA pushed meaningfully toward a passing prediction, and your choice of full-time study aligned with the vast majority of successful students at 92.7%. Overall, while some elements like your GPA and LSAT highlighted your potential, the lower decile rankings in school grades carried the most weight in tipping the scales toward this outcome, especially when compared to peers who typically rank higher in those areas. This doesn't define your abilities—many students overcome similar hurdles. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking ways to improve your school rankings through tutoring or advanced courses, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that speak to your resilience as a Black woman in this field. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or bridge courses designed for diverse applicants. Keep pushing forward; your journey is inspiring, and with targeted improvements, law school doors can open wider. We're rooting for you.
